questions.question: Define and explain Inorder Traversal with an example. For a tree with root A, left child B (with left child D), and right child C, give the output.
keyPoints: Inorder: Left → Root → Right
Visit left subtree first, then root, then right
For tree: D → B → A → C
Shows the recursive nature of traversal

questions.question: Write the algorithm for Preorder Traversal and state one practical application where it is used.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Visit Root, then Recurse Left, then Recurse Right
Practical applications: Creating copy of tree, prefix expression generation
Time complexity: O(n), Space: O(h)

questions.question: Explain Level-order traversal with the help of a queue. Also state why it is called Breadth-First Search.
keyPoints: Uses FIFO Queue data structure
Enqueue root, process, enqueue children
Visits level by level, left to right
BFS because it explores breadth before depth

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of tree traversals? Explain with justification.
keyPoints: Time: O(n) - each node visited exactly once
Space: O(h) where h is height
Worst case (skewed tree): O(n) space
Balanced tree: O(log n) space

questions.question: Differentiate between Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) traversals in binary trees.
keyPoints: DFS: Inorder, Preorder, Postorder - goes deep before wide
BFS: Level-order - explores all neighbors before moving deeper
DFS uses stack (or recursion), BFS uses queue
DFS suitable for deep trees, BFS for finding shortest path

questions.question: Why is Inorder traversal preferred for Binary Search Trees? Explain with reason.
keyPoints: Inorder visits nodes in sorted ascending order
Left subtree contains smaller values, right subtree contains larger
Useful for: sorting, searching, validation

questions.question: Explain Postorder traversal and state its applications in computer science.
keyPoints: Order: Left → Right → Root
Root visited last
Applications: Deleting tree (free memory), evaluating postfix expressions, finding file system sizes

questions.question:
Write pseudocode for recursive implementations of all three depth-first traversals (Inorder, Preorder, Postorder) and trace them on the following tree:
    1
   / \
  2   3
 / \
4   5
keyPoints: Write recursive algorithm for Inorder: Inorder(node) → Inorder(left) → print → Inorder(right)
Write recursive algorithm for Preorder: Preorder(node) → print → Preorder(left) → Preorder(right)
Write recursive algorithm for Postorder: Postorder(node) → Postorder(left) → Postorder(right) → print
Trace: Inorder: 4, 2, 5, 1, 3 | Preorder: 1, 2, 4, 5, 3 | Postorder: 4, 5, 2, 3, 1

questions.question: Write and explain the algorithm for Level-order traversal using a queue. Trace it on a sample tree and explain why this traversal is different from DFS traversals.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Initialize queue, enqueue root, while queue not empty: dequeue, process, enqueue left if exists, enqueue right if exists
Uses Queue instead of Stack/recursion
Different from DFS: visits level by level vs depth-first
Also called Breadth-First Search (BFS)
Application: Finding shortest path in unweighted graph

questions.question: Explain how you would reconstruct a binary tree if you are given its Preorder and Inorder traversals. Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Preorder: Root, Left subtree, Right subtree (gives root first)
Inorder: Left, Root, Right (helps identify left/right subtrees)
Find root from preorder in inorder, split into left and right parts
Recursively reconstruct left and right subtrees
Example: Preorder: ABDCE, Inorder: BADC E → Tree: root A, left B, right C with D as C's left child

questions.question: Compare iterative and recursive approaches for tree traversal. Discuss advantages, disadvantages, and the data structures used in each approach.
keyPoints: Recursive: Uses call stack, simpler code, O(h) space
Iterative: Uses explicit stack (for DFS) or queue (for BFS), no recursion limit
Recursive easier to write but may cause stack overflow for deep trees
Iterative more efficient for very deep trees, uses extra memory explicitly
Time complexity same: O(n) for both
